teamObjective.explanation: I hold only low, non-trump spades and have the opening lead. The best team plan is to play a very low single so my partner (player 2) can win the trick cheaply and take over the lead, since I am unlikely to retain control myself.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 cards and no public plays are known. They are likely to hold at least one card higher than a 3 of spades or a trump, giving them a reasonable chance to take this trick if the opponents do not overcall. I treat this as probabilistic, not certain.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each have 3 cards and no cards have been played. They may hold higher non-trump singles or trumps, so any low lead I make could be captured. However, leading the lowest card minimizes the value I surrender if they win.

actionRationale.primaryReason: play-single-spade-3-copy1 is the lowest card in my hand. It sheds my weakest card, asks partner to take the trick, and keeps the 4, 5, and 6 for later if the trick comes back around or for a follow-up play.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 of spades is my highest non-trump card and still very beatable. Leading it wastes the only modestly higher card I have and leaves me with even weaker follow-ups.
The 5 is higher than the 3 but still low; it does not materially improve my chance of winning the trick and removes a middle card I might need later.
The 4 is only slightly higher than the 3 and is equally vulnerable to opponent high cards or trumps; the 3 is a strictly cheaper lead that conveys the same signal to partner.

risks: An opponent may have a higher non-trump single or any trump and capture the lead.
Partner may not be able to beat the opponents, causing the opposing team to gain control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the absolute lowest card minimizes the information and card value I give away, preserves my slightly higher spades, and positions the hand so that partner, not me, is best placed to win the trick.

notes: Opening lead with a weak hand; selected the minimum card to support a partner-takeover strategy.
